speaker: interviewer
text: --Norman Tuckman. [BACKROUND NOISE] The interviewer is Leonie Lachmish. And the date is October the 30th, 1996. Norman, you were just telling us about the wonderful Seder night you had in the RAF. Where did it take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nijmegan, in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: At an army base.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not an army base. It was a town. The Army and the Air Force was scattered around in the hills and the fields. And we turned up in whatever we could find-- trucks, Bren Gun carriers, motorcycle. But we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in uniform, our best uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember where the Seder took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right in the center of Nijmegan. The Royal Air Force had taken an empty shop and laid it out with beautiful white tablecloth. We had forgotten what all this looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other experiences did you have as a Jewish soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we moved up, we came to Lille. Towards--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lille in France. Lille, in north of France. We-- it was the High Holidays, so I was again called and informed that in a few days the Jewish personnel would be celebrating the High Holidays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- I cannot recall. One of us, or somebody informed me that there was a synagogue, and I should go and look at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I set off with military police to look at the synagogue in Lille, which we duly found. It was cluttered up, again, with old furniture. I reported back to group that there was old furniture. And they said, what would I need to bring it up in order? So I assessed, and I said I needed approximately 10 or 15 or 20 men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they dispatched to me 20 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners of war, German prisoners, who duly came and scrubbed and cleaned the synagogue. And I think the synagogue has never been so clean before or since. And came the High Holidays, from nowhere, Jews appeared. And we had a wonderful service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: French. All French. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had been happening with them all these years? The war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have no idea. I couldn't-- my French was rather limited to [FRENCH] and-- I was a yes man. I never learned any French. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just prayed together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other experiences as a Jewish soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against our non-Jewish, as we moved further into-- we crossed into Germany, again, one night I was called out, as the infantry in front of us had overrun a displaced persons camp. That meant prisoners or people taken there as slave labor. The Air Force sent me ahead to find out what the position was. I went again with military police and medical. But there's very little that we could do. It was the most tragic sight. It was not a concentration camp, but slave labor. Children, hundreds if not thousands of children, all with their hair shaven off. You could barely tell what was a boy or girl. They were collected from all countries to be slave labor, and so were the men and women. As we opened the doors, some were sleeping and some were dying. You couldn't-- I couldn't possibly do anything at all. They were all louse-infected, sick. We signaled back that we were locking the gates and we await reinforcements in the morning. And so we did. We stood at the gate. We didn't leave anybody in or out. And in the morning we handed it over to our good friend from the infantry, who took over. They had medical staff. They had army staff. And they took over, and we moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people families? Were they families? Were the children and parents connected?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not connected. There were just hundreds of children in barrack huts. And there were men, separated from women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what origin were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. Yugoslavs, anything. You mention it, they were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all-- and at this time, did it bring your thoughts closer to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We immediately thought, what's happening with our families? What's happening with our-- but there was no answer. You lived from day to day. You accepted what was being presented, and there-- there was no time. And you couldn't ask any question. There was nobody to ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after this, this was in 1945--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You moved on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved on. And war soon finished. And from then onwards, we moved forward and forward. And when war finished, we were actually a support squadron to an infantry brigade. And we finished up right top of Germany, if you know the map, in Schleswig, called Flensburg. And there the war finished. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: War finished, and for some weeks I made myself busy doing nothing. Or so to say, the Air Force had no further duties to perform, except I was attached to the police on security work, on simple security work, transporting of German prisoners who came down from Denmark. Picking up bodies, and all sorts of regular work. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of bodies? What do you mean picking up bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we also had-- people got drowned. People had accidents. And all sorts of regular work. People had to be sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then job came-- my squadron leader in-- in charge asked me if I would like to go into Denmark. He had a job for me. I said, Denmark? Lovely. Which month are we in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now at the end of the war, and we're going now into the first two or three months after '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: May, June, July.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We are now going towards the end of '45, September, October '45. I moved into Denmark, into Kolding. I was attached to the Danish police. And for-- for me, Denmark was a-- a new experience. Denmark was a food larder for the Germans. They had absolutely everything, and we had nothing. Our life was a life of luxury. I actually slept in a bed. I was attached to the police, and I was billeted with a Danish family. And we had a wonderful time, a wonderful time. We were feted. We were taken out. They couldn't do enough for us. I loved the Danes, and they loved us. But it was a-- a love affair that didn't last long. Not because they were hostile. No, because I was foolish. We had served-- we had served right through the war. And our life was very basic and very simple. And our food was very simple. And suddenly I was confronted with that huge amount of rich food that my stomach couldn't take it. And within a very few weeks, I was ill, so ill I couldn't sit up in my office. I [? brought ?] myself a little camp bed, and I was soon discovered and sent to a local doctor, who said, no, no, you better go and see the Air Force doctor. And the Air Force doctor was four hours away in an open jeep. And by the time I arrived in December 1945, I was half dead. And he looked at me and said, no, we fly you back to base hospital. Next morning, they flew me back to base hospital, which was in Hamburg. And I had an internal hemorrhage. I had an ulcer that burst, and I was seriously ill for some days. Once bleeding stopped, they picked me up and sent me back to base hospital in Swindon, and I was in hospital here back in England in the ward with 72 other fools like me who suffered from gastric ulcer. And that was pretty well the end of my Air Force life. When I came out of hospital, I returned to bomber command. But bomber command said, there isn't much work for you here, Sergeant, we send you to transport command.

speaker: interviewer
text: And transport command said, well, it's nice meeting you, but it's best for you [LAUGHS] to take your discharge, because you were going to leave the Air Force in any case. The war was finished. We were waiting to be discharged, yes? And so I came out a little bit earlier, but nothing in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came out with a pension. My pension was five shillings a week, which is not a lot of money. It wasn't much then. It's nothing now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was your demobilization.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my demobilization. I came out with a nice new suit, and we got 100 pounds if I remember rightly, and a clean shirt. That was my [? demob. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, did you yet know about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this point, I learned from-- [INAUDIBLE] of course we had learned from [HEBREW], our great sorrows, our parents. We didn't know. Everybody was trying to get in touch with the Red Cross, letters forming. We didn't know. But [HEBREW], we learned that our parents had either not survived or yes survived. But we didn't know. But after a while, I was notified by the Red Cross that my father and my mother and my sister had not survived. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, will you tell us what happened to them? And repeat their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was taken to Buchenwald concentration camp, [? and ?] so we are told he was there from 1940 to 1942. And he was a tailor. He worked as tailor in the concentration camp, as a tailor. But in charge of his hut was a Polish non-Jew. Every [? huge ?] hut had somebody in charge of the hut. And he had smuggled a Polish paper in to him. And so it appears that he was found to have the Polish paper, and that was an absolutely most serious offense. And they ask him who smuggled the paper, and he said my father. My father was taken out and shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan. Nathan [? Naftali. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when in 1942?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know exactly. We don't know exactly, but it was towards the end of '42. My mother-- my mother was taken in 19-- in April '42. My mother had left her flat, and by then taken over the management of the old age home in Munich. She looked after the old people. And my sister and my mother lived in the old age home.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish old age home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish old age home. In from March-- April 1942 when the final liquidation took place, they were all taken to a place in Poland. And nobody survived. That we learned. I learned after I came out the Air Force.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tuchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fanny. And your sister was how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- 1929-- would have been 15, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't know how they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What has been described to me that many perished on transport. They didn't survive the transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other family, extended family? Grandparents, aunts, uncles, friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: All that I had, all that I had, all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one cousin who survived in America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But out of our close family, there are no survivors at all. Is there anyone else's name you'd like to mention on this cassette? Grandparents' names? Well, those grandparents that hadn't died before, no, nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you actually find this information out for sure?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from the year between 1946 through '47, after I left the Air Force and returned to civilian life. For some month I was so unsettled, not-- not only physically unsettled, but mentally, I couldn't do anything. I couldn't work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But life must go on, and so it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it that gave you the strength to carry on?

speaker: interviewee
text: One has no choice. I had a commitment. I returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What commitment are you talking about? What commitment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Commitment to life, as such. One must-- life must go on. I realized life must go on. We had nothing. Life wasn't as full as it is today. I found myself lodgings. I lived with nice family, non-Jewish family, who looked after me very well. I soon made friends. I lived near a hospital, so there were plenty of nurses. We had a great circle of friends. We had a lot of fun. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I found work. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for an electrical firm as a representative, but again, after some month, I really considered myself unemployable. For some reason, it didn't appeal to me, and not much else I could find. I tried many, many other jobs. I wasn't qualified. I hadn't learned anything much. I had no [? quick ?] qualifications. So the only thing to start my own business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what could I possibly start? I hadn't any money, and I hadn't any knowledge. But I knew-- one knows people, meet people. And I knew a good friend of mine who had a tobacconist shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew very little about tobacco. I didn't smoke. Or when I did smoke, I gave it up. But I started a tobacconist, confectionery business, did quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London, in southeast London. But one thing that came to my mind whilst I was sick in an Air Force hospital, there used to be a trolley came round. And he brought the trolley, manned by local volunteers, and served us with all the little necessities of daily life. And I thought that was a wonderful [? tie, ?] because I hadn't anybody to visit me in hospital. So all the things I used to buy there. And I helped the woman who brought the trolley round. Now, when I started my business in London, I thought what a good idea that would be. And that's what I did. I approached a local hospital if I could do that. And they said, yes, do that. And we designed a special trolley. And that's how the trolley shop service started in London, in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would go with a trolley.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a trolley, a specially built trolley, that was lockable, that contained cigarettes, tobacco, chocolates, biscuits, face flannels, toothpaste, all the things that you need in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then people didn't-- didn't know how dangerous cigarettes were.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had cigarette machines downstairs, [LAUGHS] everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you yourself would go round the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. I had my shop to look after. But I employed part-time worker, and from that one trolley, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a number-- a number of trolleys started. Many hospitals came to us, and I came to hospital. Eventually we had 12 or 13 hospitals to serve.

speaker: interviewer
text: From then, we had to have a warehouse that supplied these hospitals. From the hospitals, we had hospital shops, little shops situated in hospitals, who opened like an ordinary little shop. And from the hospital shop came the hospital canteen, because the hospital management said, well, you do it so well. Why not do our canteen? And I had two large canteens, supplied hundreds of sandwiches every day. Which hospitals were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: St. George's Hospital Hyde Park Corner, St. George's Hospital Tooting, Dulwich Hospital, St. Giles Hospital, South London Hospital, St. Helier Hospital, all over London. It was a wonderful experience. It served me well. But my love-- my love was property. Always been my ambition, and so eventually I sold my business and returned to property. And I became an estate agent. But not an estate agent as you understand it, but a managing agent, a man who manages an estate or works for an insurance company, or works for the bank. And that's the work we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that involve, that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Involved managing. As an example, insurance company buys a block of flats. They don't do any work. They're not really concerned, all they're concerned is their check. But the managing agent looks after the gardening, the lifts, the letting. Everything that goes on-- collecting, selling, everything. That's what we did. And eventually I had a managing portfolio of about 300-odd dwellings to look after.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start with this new business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there we're going back in early '60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, by then you had met your wife. Tell about your wife and how you met her.

words.text: your wife and how you met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I met my wife by accident or incident. I was introduced. Fortunately, she accepted me. And in 1952, we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your wife from? Where is your wife from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife lived the other side of London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she from originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came from eastern Germany. Totally different part, and totally different family. They were German Jews, and had we lived in Germany, I doubt if we would ever have met.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Bayer, like the aspirin people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth. Tell me, how do you think the Holocaust has affected your philosophy in life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust affected us in-- in a way that one must always-- we always look back and say, why did I come out? Why was God kind enough, good enough to spare me, and not my parents, who were much better, better in every respect? Why did they all perish and I lived on? This is a question that I will take with me to my grave. There's no answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Likewise, why did I go to war and came back, and many of my colleagues did not turn for breakfast? When I came for breakfast and I sat down and I said, where is Jim? I'm sorry, he did not come back last night. He did not return to base. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Why were they missing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Questions we can't answer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God was kind to me. God spared me. I came perhaps to give this message. Perhaps to educate my children to live good Jews, to be good citizens, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to be kind, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to take something out of their life, a word that is hate. Never hate. Never hate another person. You may not like him. You may even dislike him. But don't hate him. Because hate produced the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has the Holocaust affected decisions you've made in your own life, that you think you might not have made that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The decision for me was if possible, eventually-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no. I can't say to live in Israel, because that came much later. No. Just to remember. Remember. As we say in the Torah, [HEBREW]. Don't forget, ever, what happened to our people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't hear about your children. Tell us about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two good children. One in Haifa and one in Yerushalayim. And I have six grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Judith, and one is Jeanette.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Their husbands' names, [? actually. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The children, my oldest daughter in Haifa, her husband's name is [? Leo. ?] Children are Leah-- Leah, Mira, and Avi, [? Avraham. ?] And the daughter in Yerushalayim, Jeanette and Arthur. Arthur works at the [HEBREW]. And today he's at our embassy in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's in foreign office.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's rather a coincidence we are here. And they are for four years in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children. The children [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? Dorat. ?] Doron. [? Dorat, ?] Doron. Doron. And Leeor. And Noam. Noam, unfortunately, is handicapped. And he's here in an institution, Beit Uri. We are very grateful to Beit Uri because he's so well looked after. So well looked after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Norman, is there anything else you'd like to add as a message for the generations to come, to your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: All that I've said to be good citizens to Israel, to live as good Jews, and always remember what happened to us. We are not an example, but just to remember what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewer
text: --for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to introduce, this is my wife. Not the better-- not the better half, the best part of my life. She's put up with me for 44 years, and all I can say, my deepest sympathy. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we wish you to 120.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we say in Israel, to 125. And when people say, why 125? The other-- other five years to pay off your [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: To pay off your mortgage. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at the end of '37 or '38. My father, mother, sister, and myself. This picture is taken with my mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a special relationship between my mother-in-law and myself, as I had no mother, I treated her and called her mother. She's smiling and laughing. I'm sure she didn't know what she was smiling on, looking at me. This is a picture taken in Israel. Of course, we came to Israel in December '85. And this is one of our early pictures taken here, with four grandchildren, our two daughters, and one son-in-law. This is the fifth grandchild, Noam, regretfully handicapped. Very much esteemed and beloved by us all, now in a home in Israel, Beit Uri.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Noam is now 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the picture with him there?

speaker: interviewee
text: With him, my daughter Jeanette. This is my wife with five of our grandchildren, Leah, Mira, Leeor, Avi, and Doron, in our garden in Ashkelon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Norman, I want to thank you again. And on behalf of the Spielberg Foundation, we want to wish you only simchas and joyful occasions, and much joy from your children and your grandchildren and your dear wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Todah. Thank you, Leonie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're very happy to present a collection of Norman's medals and souvenirs from his service in the Royal Air Force, which he was too modest for us to show in his presence. So well done, Norman.
